briefing.opening: Good morning. I will now begin the Climate Change Daily Briefing for June 1, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Worsening Climate Crisis and the Reality of Policy Delays
sections.content: The UN reiterated the severity of the climate crisis, warning that the global average temperature will rise by 1.9 degrees Celsius before 2030 and that record high temperatures will continue for the next five years [6]. These warnings indicate that climate change is fueling more extreme weather [46], and domestically, this is manifesting as blurring seasonal boundaries, with heatwaves expected in the central region and heavy rainfall in the southern region from early June [39]. Specifically, Jeonbuk Self-Governing Province activated an emergency response headquarters for summer forest pest and disease outbreaks [4], and the need for changes in maritime safety standards has also been raised [7], increasing concerns about direct damage to local communities. However, despite these climate disasters becoming a reality, the Carbon Neutrality Act remains stalled in the National Assembly due to disagreements between ruling and opposition parties, missing its amendment deadline [5, 12, 13]. Even though Bank of Korea Governor Rhee Chang-yong acknowledged the risks of the climate crisis [11], the blueprint for policy implementation remains unclear.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Efforts and Active Local Community Participation
sections.content: Various domestic efforts and achievements to address the climate crisis are also continuing. Yongin City achieved a remarkable 95% of its targets in the first implementation review of its Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth Basic Plan, reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 320,000 tons [14, 16, 17, 18]. Seogwipo City encouraged voluntary participation from citizens by distributing a total of 140 million KRW in incentives to participants in its Carbon Neutrality Point System [19]. Furthermore, the Korea Meteorological Administration and the National Climate Change Response Committee are exploring ways to strengthen Korea's role in global climate change research [2], and Hope Friends Korea Food for the Hungry is holding a climate change response project competition in 2026 [1], demonstrating active national and private-sector response efforts. Education to raise environmental awareness among future generations is also actively underway. Jeju youth are responding to the climate crisis using data and AI technologies [3], Changwon City and Yeoncheon County are operating 'Carbon Neutrality ECO-UP Education' and 'Visiting Carbon Neutrality Education' respectively [21, 22], and Cheongju City and Seo-gu are promoting carbon reduction in daily life through carbon neutrality practice challenges and 'Green Meal' initiatives [23, 24].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and the Role of Industry
sections.content: Industries both domestically and internationally are accelerating their transition to renewable energy. The Korea Environment Corporation declared its commitment to achieving RE100 by transitioning 100% of its electricity consumption to renewable energy by 2030, strengthening its role as a key carbon neutrality institution [20, 28]. Major companies are expanding their adoption of renewable energy, with Samsung Electronics, for example, procuring renewable energy through its first direct Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) in Vietnam [27]. BEP and Hyundai Engineering & Construction are collaborating on a 1.6 gigawatt (GW) solar PPA supply, contributing to the expansion of renewable energy infrastructure [29, 36]. Taihan Electric Wire is also increasing its presence in the renewable energy grid by successively winning solar-linked projects [34]. These efforts, as seen in the European Union's (EU) case of saving 51.4 billion Euros last year through renewable energy use [30], are serving as a key driver for climate crisis response alongside economic benefits.

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Response Discussions and New Perspectives
sections.content: The international community is conducting various discussions and research to address the climate crisis. This climate conference towards COP31 is being held at a crucial time [48], and frameworks for international cooperation continue to be established, such as the roadmap process for fossil fuels and forests developed at COP30 [56]. Furthermore, the EJN Climate Change Media Partnership is recruiting reporting fellowships for coverage of COP31 in 2026 [60], emphasizing the importance of disseminating climate change information. Interestingly, researchers in Connecticut are studying whether virtual reality (VR) can increase people's engagement with climate change [52], and discussions are also taking place on how philanthropy can contribute to solving climate change and health issues in local communities [42]. However, on the other hand, the increasing mental health impact due to the climate crisis, such as 'eco-paralysis,' is being warned against [54], and criticisms of a lack of public interest in climate change have been raised in some regions [55, 59]. Thus, the psychological and social challenges of climate crisis response cannot be overlooked.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing acknowledged the severity of the climate crisis and the reality of policy delays, while also highlighting various carbon neutrality efforts and positive movements towards renewable energy transition both domestically and internationally. This is a time when continuous discussions and new approaches from the international community are becoming even more crucial for overcoming the climate crisis. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Heavy rain due to typhoon affecting Jeju and southern coast; Seoul metropolitan area up to 33 degrees Celsius... easing after the 4th. Strong rain is expected in Jeju and the southern regions until the 2nd due to the influence of Typhoon Jangmi, the 6th typhoon of the season, which is moving northward from the waters southwest of Okinawa, Japan. The high-temperature, high-humidity water vapor brought by the typhoon is clashing with dry air, leading to expected heavy rainfall warnings in the southern regions. The Korea Meteorological Administration held an ad-hoc forecast briefing on the morning of the 1st, stating, "From the south..."

summary.phenomenon: Heavy rainfall is expected in Jeju and the southern coastal areas due to a typhoon, with temperatures in the Seoul metropolitan area reaching up to 33 degrees Celsius before easing after the 4th. Strong rain will fall in Jeju and the southern regions until the 2nd, influenced by Typhoon Jangmi, the 6th typhoon, moving northward from southwest of Okinawa, Japan. The high-temperature, high-humidity vapor carried by the typhoon is encountering dry air, leading to heavy rain warnings for the southern provinces.
